chunks.text: FAQ
chunks.title: FAQ

headings: Does opendetail ship a chat UI?
chunks.text:
Does opendetail ship a chat UI?

No. opendetail is the engine. The current package focuses on the build step, local index, runtime, and Next.js adapter. If you want a chat surface, you bring that separately.
chunks.title: FAQ

chunks.text:
Is this built on OpenAI?

Yes. opendetail uses the OpenAI Responses API underneath. The product value is the setup flow, grounding model, local retrieval layer, and cleaner integration surface around that API.
chunks.title: FAQ

headings: Do I need a vector database or external search service?
chunks.text:
Do I need a vector database or external search service?

No. Today the default flow builds a local JSON index and searches it locally. That keeps the setup small and easy to reason about.
chunks.title: FAQ

chunks.text:
What content gets indexed?

Whatever matches the include globs in opendetail.toml, minus anything in exclude. The main target is Markdown and MDX content that already explains your product clearly.
chunks.title: FAQ

headings: Does MDX still work if the file uses components?
chunks.text:
Does MDX still work if the file uses components?

Yes, but the important part is the text. opendetail keeps useful MDX text and headings; imports, exports, and MDX-only syntax are not the point of retrieval.
chunks.title: FAQ

headings: Can it answer from outside my docs?
chunks.text:
Can it answer from outside my docs?

No. The runtime is designed to answer only from the retrieved sources. It should cite those sources instead of filling gaps with general knowledge.
chunks.title: FAQ

headings: What happens when the docs are not enough?
chunks.text:
What happens when the docs are not enough?

It responds plainly that the topic is not documented yet or cannot be confirmed from the configured docs. The point is still the same: fail clearly instead of improvising.
chunks.title: FAQ

chunks.text:
Where does the data live?

The built index lives in your app at .opendetail/index.json. At runtime, opendetail loads that local index and sends only the retrieved sections to the model. In self-hosted, that means your runtime needs OPENAI_API_KEY. In hosted, the client transport points at a hosted endpoint instead.
chunks.title: FAQ

chunks.text:
What are the integration modes?

There are two documented modes:

self-hosted: scaffold a route and keep OPENAI_API_KEY in your own runtimehosted: skip route scaffolding and point your client transport at a hosted endpoint later

The request and stream contract stay the same across both modes.
chunks.title: FAQ

headings: Is the hosted backend available yet?
chunks.text:
Is the hosted backend available yet?

No. In this phase, the hosted path is documented and scaffolded only. The OpenDetail-managed proxy backend is not shipped yet.
chunks.title: FAQ

chunks.text:
What frameworks are supported today?

Today the package ships a core Node runtime and a thin Next.js adapter via opendetail/next. That means Next.js is the first framework-specific path right now, while the runtime itself stays framework-light.
chunks.title: FAQ

chunks.text:
Is setup still manual?

The CLI includes a setup flow (opendetail setup) that can scaffold config, route wiring for self-hosted, and an initial index. It also includes a diagnostics command (opendetail doctor) for fast verification in both integration modes.
chunks.title: FAQ

headings: Is it only for developer docs?
chunks.text:
Is it only for developer docs?

No. Developer docs are a natural fit, but the shape also works for product guides, onboarding, help content, and internal knowledge. If the content is in Markdown and MDX and answers real questions, it can fit.
chunks.title: FAQ

chunks.text:
Does it replace docs?

No. opendetail works because the docs or knowledge content already exist. It makes that material easier to ask, retrieve, and cite inside an app.
chunks.title: FAQ

chunks.text:
How It Works

opendetail follows a small two-part flow: build once from your Markdown and MDX, then answer questions from the resulting local index. The design stays simple on purpose so the product remains easy to reason about and easy to inspect.
chunks.title: How It Works

chunks.text:
Build time

You can start with opendetail setup, which scaffolds the defaults most teams need. In self-hosted mode, that means config, route file, and initial index. In hosted mode, it means config and initial index, while skipping the route file on purpose. Under the hood, both flows still center on opendetail.toml, where you define which Markdown and MDX files to include, which ones to exclude, and which base path should be used when source URLs are generated.

When you run the build command, opendetail reads the matched files and turns them into a local index at .opendetail/index.json. Content is split by heading sections so the runtime can retrieve smaller, more relevant pieces instead of entire files. The output is local and file-based, which keeps the setup light and makes the index easy to inspect.
chunks.title: How It Works

chunks.text:
Request time

At request time, the runtime loads the local index into memory and searches it for the most relevant sections. Today that retrieval step is lexical and local. There is no separate vector database, sync layer, or external search service in the default flow.

Once the relevant sections are selected, opendetail sends only those sections to the OpenAI Responses API along with strict instructions: answer only from the provided sources, cite factual claims, and do not invent missing information. That is what makes it a grounded assistant rather than a general chat layer.
chunks.title: How It Works

chunks.text:
Runtime shape

The current package is server-first. You can use the core runtime directly in Node, or use the thin Next.js adapter that exposes a route handler with a one-line createNextRoute() helper. In Next.js, that route must run on the Node.js runtime.

Responses can be streamed, and the response shape includes source information alongside the generated answer. The request shape stays { question: string }, and the stream contract stays meta, sources, images, delta, done, error across both integration modes. If the configured docs do not contain enough information, opendetail answers clearly that the topic is not documented yet or cannot be confirmed instead of pretending it knows more than the content says.
chunks.title: How It Works

chunks.text:
Integration split

self-hosted is the current end-to-end backend path. You scaffold a route, keep OPENAI_API_KEY in your own runtime, and let your server call the model.

hosted is the setup-focused path. In this phase, it is documented and scaffolded, but the OpenDetail-managed proxy backend is not shipped yet. The point of the split is to make that future setup path explicit now without changing the grounding model or the request contract later.
chunks.title: How It Works

chunks.text:
What this design buys you

This design keeps the product small, inspectable, and honest. Your content stays the source of truth. The local index stays close to the app. The answer path stays narrow enough that a reader can understand where the response came from and why.
chunks.title: How It Works

chunks.text:
Overview

opendetail is a grounded assistant layer for apps that already have useful Markdown and MDX content. It turns that content into answers that stay tied to your own source material instead of drifting into general chat.
chunks.title: Overview

chunks.text:
What opendetail is

At its core, opendetail is a thin product layer on top of the OpenAI Responses API. You point it at content you already maintain, build a local index, and let your app answer questions from that material. The goal is simple: make your product easier to understand without moving your content into a separate platform or asking a team to rebuild what they already wrote.

The current package stays intentionally small. It ships the runtime, setup-focused CLI commands, and thin framework wiring helpers. That makes it a good fit when you want grounded answers inside your product, but you do not want to stand up extra retrieval plumbing or build a custom Responses integration from scratch.
chunks.title: Overview

chunks.text:
Where it fits

opendetail fits best when the hard part is not "how do we make a chatbot," but "how do we ship a grounded answer flow with less setup." It works well for product explainers, help content, onboarding material, internal guides, and developer docs that already live in Markdown and MDX.

It is especially useful when a team wants a narrow assistant that can answer common product questions, cite its sources, and fail plainly when the answer is not in the content. That shape is often more useful than a broader assistant that sounds confident but cannot show where an answer came from.
chunks.title: Overview

chunks.text:
Who it is for

opendetail is for teams that:

already have product knowledge in Markdown and MDXwant grounded answers inside an app, route, or hosted transportcare more about clarity and citations than open-ended chatprefer a local index and a small integration surface

It can serve technical and non-technical content alike, as long as the source material is clear enough to answer real questions. The product is only as useful as the content behind it, which is why clear headings, concrete language, and maintained source files matter so much here.
chunks.title: Overview

chunks.text:
What it is not

opendetail is not a full assistant platform or a general-purpose agent framework. It does not try to replace the OpenAI Responses API. It packages the setup, grounding, and integration ergonomics around that API. It also does not replace your source content. It depends on it.

That boundary is part of the product. opendetail is useful because it stays close to the words you chose, the routes you own, and the questions your product actually needs to answer.
chunks.title: Overview

chunks.text:
Integration modes

| Mode | What you manage | Best for | Status |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| self-hosted | Route, runtime env, OPENAI_API_KEY | Private docs and server-owned control | Available today |
| hosted | Index build and client transport config | Faster setup and future OpenDetail-managed delivery | Documented and scaffolded in this phase |
chunks.title: Overview
